_comment: CANONICAL wire vocabulary for the phone <-> agent protocol. THE authority.
Three independent implementations speak this: the Linux agent
the app (app/lib/gamepad.ts). Adding a key to one and forgetting another is
the single most repeated defect in this repo -- the lg_commercial config
section that crash-looped a live box, and the smart-TV backends that existed
on Linux for weeks before Windows, were both this shape.
tests/test_protocol_parity.py holds all three against this file, in BOTH
directions: nothing here goes unimplemented, and nothing is accepted that
isn't here. Change this file FIRST, watch it fail, then implement.
`platforms` is load-bearing. Some vocabulary is deliberately platform-scoped
-- KDE desktop actions are meaningless on Windows, and Windows system chords
are meaningless on SteamOS. Those entries declare their platforms, and the
test asserts they are ABSENT elsewhere. That absence is a designed decision,
not an omission, and asserting it stops a half-port from looking finished.

buttons._doc: {'t':'b','k':<key>,'v':0|1}. Virtual Xbox 360 pad. Required on BOTH agents. NOTE: the d-pad keys are a LATCHED ABS hat on Linux, not edge-triggered -- one missing v:0 pins the axis forever (see tests/test_dpad_latch.py).

specialKeys._doc: {'t':'k','k':<key>} named non-character keys. Required on BOTH agents.

desktopKeys._doc: SteamOS/Bazzite (KDE Plasma) desktop actions on the {t:'k'} channel: 'meta' taps Super (Kickoff), 'overview' fires the KWin overview chord. LINUX ONLY by design -- the app gates them on caps.desktop and an agent that doesn't know the name drops the frame.

chords._doc: One-shot Windows desktop system shortcuts the agent expands into a key sequence. WINDOWS ONLY by design. Ctrl+Alt+Del is deliberately absent: it is the Secure Attention Sequence and SendInput cannot generate it.

frameTypes._doc: Top-level {'t':...} discriminators the gamepad WebSocket accepts.
